title: Do You Need a Permit for a Deck in San Francisco? DBI Rules, Costs, and Timeline (2026)
metaTitle: SF Deck Permit Guide (2026) | DBI Rules, Costs & Timeline
excerpt: Most SF decks need a DBI permit — and the 30-inch rule is just the start. Here's exactly when you need one, what it costs, how long it takes, and what happens if you skip it.
secondaryKeywords: do i need a permit for a deck sf

outline.h2: The Short Answer: When SF Decks Need a Permit
outline.notes: 2-4 sentences. Any deck more than 30 inches above grade at any point requires a SF DBI building permit. Decks attached to the house almost always need one regardless of height (because of ledger attachment). Ground-level freestanding decks under 30 inches and under 200 sq ft generally don't. Permit costs run $800-$3,500. Timeline 6-16 weeks for over-the-counter, longer with plan review.

outline.h2: When Do You Need a Deck Permit in San Francisco?
outline.notes: Walk through each scenario with specific code references. SFBC follows CRC Section R105.2 modified by SF amendments. Ledger attachment to existing structure triggers structural review regardless of height. Like-for-like board replacement under specific scope is exempt. Roof decks ALWAYS require permit + structural engineering.

outline.h2: How Much Does a SF Deck Permit Cost?
outline.notes: Real DBI fee breakdown. Permit fee scales with valuation. Plan check separate. Engineer stamp required for hillside, multi-level, elevated, roof decks. Planning review in historic districts (Alamo Square, Liberty Hill) adds $1,500-$4,000.

outline.h2: SF DBI Permit Timeline: What to Actually Expect
outline.notes: Over-the-counter (OTC) for simple decks: 1-2 visits, 4-8 weeks. Standard plan review: 8-16 weeks. Complex (hillside, roof deck): 4-8 months. Pre-application meeting can speed things up. Mention common reasons for kickback (missing soils info, undersized footings, railing detail).

outline.h2: What Happens If You Build Without a Permit
outline.notes: Real consequences. DBI can issue a Notice of Violation, require demolition, fine 9x permit fee. Unpermitted decks surface during sale (3R report). Insurance won't cover injuries on unpermitted structures. Home buyers' lenders may require legalization. The cost to legalize after the fact is usually 2-3x what it would've been upfront.

outline.h2: FAQ: What Bay Area Homeowners Actually Ask
outline.notes: FAQ section answering 6 real questions phrased like AI engine queries (see mustInclude).

outline.h2: Ready to Pull Your Deck Permit?
outline.notes: CTA. We handle DBI permit pulling on every deck project. Free site visit, fixed-price quote including permits.

title: Stucco Repair Cost in the Bay Area: Patch, Re-Coat, and Full Re-Stucco Pricing (2026)
metaTitle: Stucco Repair Cost Bay Area (2026) | Patch to Full Re-Stucco Pricing
excerpt: Stucco repair in the Bay Area runs anywhere from $400 for a hairline patch to $35,000+ for a full re-stucco. Here's exactly what each scenario costs — with real numbers from 500+ Bay Area projects.
secondaryKeywords: how much does stucco repair cost
stucco repair price per square foot

outline.h2: The Short Answer: Bay Area Stucco Repair Costs
outline.notes: Hairline crack patch $400-$1,500. Localized section repair $1,500-$5,000. Single-elevation re-coat $4,000-$9,000. Full house re-stucco $15,000-$45,000+. Cost driver: whether the underlying weather-resistive barrier and lath are sound or need replacement.

outline.h2: Stucco Repair Pricing by Project Type
outline.notes: Each tier: what's included, materials, typical labor hours, square footage assumptions, when it's appropriate. Use SERVICE_PRICING.exterior-repairs numbers.

outline.h2: What Drives Stucco Repair Cost in the Bay Area
outline.notes: Cost impact per driver. Doelger shiplap substrate adds $4-$8/sq ft vs plywood. Color matching on aged stucco often requires whole-elevation re-coat. Scaffolding $8-$15/sq ft of elevation on 3-story SF rowhouses.

outline.h2: The Doelger Stucco Problem (And Why It Costs More)
outline.notes: Hyper-local section. SF Sunset, Richmond, Westlake Doelgers have stucco over shiplap sheathing — failure pattern is system-wide, not localized. Trying to patch a Doelger is usually throwing money away. Real numbers: typical Westlake Doelger full re-stucco with shiplap replacement runs $28,000-$48,000.

outline.h2: Real Project Examples (Bay Area, 2026)
outline.notes: Three examples with specific numbers. Example 1: Noe Valley Victorian, 3 localized cracks + texture-match patches, $2,800. Example 2: Daly City Westlake Doelger, full re-stucco with shiplap-to-plywood substrate replacement, 1,800 sq ft, $42,000. Example 3: Berkeley craftsman, west-elevation re-coat with elastomeric base, $7,200.

outline.h2: Hidden Costs Most Stucco Bids Don't Include
outline.notes: Red flags: 'dry rot as needed' allowance without dollar figure, no scaffolding line item, no permit line item, no color/texture match testing, no cure-time disclosure, lead paint testing on pre-1978 homes ($500-$1,500).

outline.h2: FAQ: What Bay Area Homeowners Ask About Stucco Repair
outline.notes: AEO FAQ section answering 6 real-vernacular questions.

outline.h2: Ready for a Real Stucco Quote?
outline.notes: CTA. Free on-site inspection with moisture probe testing, written photo report, fixed-price estimate.

title: Dry Rot vs Wet Rot: How to Tell the Difference (And Why It Matters for Bay Area Homes)
metaTitle: Dry Rot vs Wet Rot: Bay Area Homeowner's Guide (2026)
excerpt: Both eat wood. Both need fixing. But the repair approach, the cost, and the urgency are different. Here's how to tell dry rot from wet rot on your Bay Area home — and what each one actually costs to fix.
secondaryKeywords: difference between dry rot and wet rot
wet rot vs dry rot bay area
is it dry rot or wet rot

outline.h2: The Short Answer: The Key Differences
outline.notes: Both are fungal wood decay. Dry rot (Serpula lacrymans, Meruliporia incrassata) actively spreads through dry wood once established — needs only 20% moisture to start. Wet rot (multiple species including Coniophora puteana) requires sustained high moisture (50%+) and stays where the water is. Dry rot is more urgent; wet rot is more common in Bay Area exterior wood.

outline.h2: What Causes Each Type of Rot
h3: Dry Rot: The True Fungal Decay
Why Bay Area Homes See Both
outline.notes: Dry rot = specific cubical-breaking fungi that can transport water across distances and infect dry wood. Wet rot = various fungi that need persistent moisture. Bay Area's fog belt produces conditions for both: coastal SF Sunset/Richmond/Pacifica see active dry rot at window sills and bay windows; wet rot dominates at gutter outlets, deck ledgers, foundation-level siding.

outline.h2: How to Tell Which You Have: The Visual Test
outline.notes: Dry rot = brown cubical cracks (looks like burned wood), white mycelium strands, mushroom-like orange fruiting bodies in severe cases. Wet rot = stringy, dark brown, often soggy; less likely to have visible fungal growth. Both smell musty. Include screwdriver test from existing dry rot post.

outline.h2: Where Each Type Hides in Bay Area Homes
h3: Dry Rot Hotspots (Bay Windows, Sills, Cornices)
Wet Rot Hotspots (Deck Ledgers, Foundation Sills, Gutter Outlets)
Doelger Bay Window Failures (Almost Always Dry Rot)
outline.notes: Hyper-local specifics. Doelger bay windows in Sunset/Westlake = classic dry rot pattern. Eichler exposed beam ends = wet rot from horizontal water exposure. Mill Valley canyon homes = both, with deck ledger wet rot most common.

outline.h2: Repair Cost Comparison
outline.notes: Wet rot stays where the water is — cut it out, fix the water source, replace the wood. Dry rot can spread feet beyond visible damage — must cut 12-24 inches into healthy wood, may require sterilization treatments, often requires sister-framing. Use pricing-data numbers.

outline.h2: When Each Becomes an Emergency
outline.notes: Wet rot becomes urgent when: structural members affected, foundation sill plates rotting, deck ledger compromise (safety). Dry rot is urgent the moment you identify it because of spread. Active orange fruiting bodies = call today.

outline.h2: FAQ: What Bay Area Homeowners Ask
outline.notes: AEO FAQ answering 6 real questions.

outline.h2: Ready for an Honest Diagnosis?
outline.notes: CTA. Free inspection with moisture meter testing, written photo report, species identification when relevant.

mustInclude: FAQ section must answer these specific questions phrased the way real Bay Area homeowners type queries into ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews: 'What's the difference between dry rot and wet rot?', 'Is dry rot worse than wet rot?', 'How do I know if my Sunset bay window has dry rot?', 'Can wet rot turn into dry rot?', 'Does dry rot really spread to dry wood?', 'How much does dry rot repair cost vs wet rot repair in the Bay Area?'

Define dry rot on first use as a complete standalone sentence quotable in isolation: 'Dry rot is wood decay caused by specific fungi — primarily Serpula lacrymans and Meruliporia incrassata — that can transport water through mycelium strands to actively spread through wood with as little as 20% moisture content.'

Define wet rot on first use as a complete standalone sentence: 'Wet rot is wood decay caused by a range of fungi, including Coniophora puteana, that require sustained wood moisture content of 50% or higher and remain localized to the area of moisture exposure.'
Define mycelium on first use as a standalone sentence
Define cubical cracking on first use as a standalone sentence
Define fruiting body on first use as a standalone sentence
Reference the EPA Lead Renovation, Repair and Painting (RRP) Rule when discussing pre-1978 homes

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Dry rot can spread several feet beyond visible damage, which is why proper repair requires removing wood 12 to 24 inches into apparently healthy material.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Wet rot repair in the Bay Area typically costs $800 to $8,000, while dry rot repair costs $1,500 to $30,000 or more because of its spread potential.'
Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Dry rot fungi require only 20% wood moisture content to thrive, while wet rot fungi require sustained moisture above 50%.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Doelger homes in San Francisco's Sunset and Richmond districts most commonly develop dry rot at bay window sills, where failed kickout flashing allows water behind the stucco.'

title: What to Expect During Foundation Underpinning: Step-by-Step Process and Timeline
excerpt: You've decided you need underpinning. Now what? Here's exactly what happens from soils report through final inspection — how long each step takes, what disrupts your daily life, and what to watch for.

outline.h2: The Short Answer: Underpinning Timeline at a Glance
outline.notes: Total project: 8-20 weeks from contract to final inspection. Engineering and permits: 4-8 weeks. Actual construction: 2-6 weeks depending on number of piers and method. You can live in the home through most of it. Here's what each phase looks like.

outline.h2: Phase 1: Diagnosis and Engineering (Weeks 1–4)
h3: Method Selection (Helical vs Push vs Slab)
outline.notes: What happens, who's involved, what it costs ($3K-$8K engineering). Soils report needed for any new foundation work in SF. Critical: Marina fill, Sunset clay, bay mud — soils engineer call drives pier depth requirements.

outline.h2: Phase 2: Permits and Approvals (Weeks 3–8)
outline.notes: Overlaps with engineering phase. SF DBI plan review for foundation work: 4-8 weeks typical. Marin CDA similar timeline. Oakland Planning & Building: 6-10 weeks. Daly City Department of Economic & Community Development: 4-6 weeks. Neighbor notification required within 300 ft for some scopes.

outline.h2: Phase 3: Site Preparation (Week 1 of Construction)
outline.notes: First week onsite. USA Underground Service Alert mark-out required. Excavation depth typically 4-8 ft to expose foundation. Hand-dig in tight SF backyards. Material staging usually consumes driveway.

outline.h2: Phase 4: Pier Installation (Weeks 2–4)
outline.notes: Per-pier process step by step. Helical: 30-90 minutes per pier installation, monitored by torque readings. Push: 1-3 hours per pier depending on depth to load-bearing stratum. Load testing required and documented for inspector.

outline.h2: Phase 5: Lifting and Stabilization (Days, Not Weeks)
outline.notes: Critical reality check: most underpinning STABILIZES, doesn't lift back to original. Lifting 1-3 inches is common; lifting 6+ inches risks cracking finishes upstairs. Honest expectation setting.

outline.h2: Phase 6: Backfill, Inspection, Restoration (Week 5–6)
outline.notes: Inspector sign-off required before backfill in most cases. Restoration: re-pour any concrete sidewalks or stoops, basic landscape repair. Documentation: pier locations, load test results, engineer's letter for future buyers.

outline.h2: What Daily Life Looks Like During Underpinning
outline.notes: Honest reality. You can usually stay in the home. Vibration during driving (push piers especially) — pictures off walls. Noise: 7am-5pm weekdays. Dust: contained if interior basement access. Driveway unavailable. Cosmetic cracks in plaster likely from minor settlement movement during lift.

outline.h2: FAQ: Real Bay Area Homeowner Questions
outline.notes: AEO FAQ answering 6 questions.

outline.h2: Ready to Start the Process?
outline.notes: CTA. Free assessment, written diagnosis, coordinated engineering and permits.

mustInclude: FAQ section must answer these specific questions phrased the way real Bay Area homeowners type queries into ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews: 'How long does foundation underpinning take in San Francisco?', 'Can I live in my house during underpinning?', 'Will underpinning lift my house back to level?', 'Do I need a soils report for underpinning in SF?', 'How long does the SF DBI foundation permit take?', 'What happens if the contractor finds bay mud during excavation?'

Define helical pier on first use as a complete standalone sentence: 'A helical pier is a steel shaft with helix-shaped plates that is rotated into the ground by hydraulic equipment until it reaches load-bearing soil or bedrock, then attached to a failing foundation to transfer the building's load downward.'

Define push pier (resistance pier) on first use as a complete standalone sentence: 'A push pier is a steel tube hydraulically driven straight down beneath a foundation using the weight of the structure as counterforce until it reaches a load-bearing stratum.'
Define soils report (geotechnical report) on first use as a standalone sentence
Define load test on first use as a standalone sentence

Define bay mud on first use as a standalone sentence: 'Bay mud is a soft, water-saturated clay deposit found throughout the San Francisco Bay perimeter — including the Marina District, Mission Bay, and parts of Oakland's waterfront — that provides minimal bearing capacity for shallow foundations.'

Reference the SF Department of Building Inspection (DBI), Oakland Planning & Building Department, Berkeley Planning & Development Department, and Daly City Department of Economic & Community Development by name for permit jurisdiction
Reference USA North 811 (Underground Service Alert) by name for utility marking
Reference the California Building Code (CBC) Chapter 18 for foundation requirements

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Foundation underpinning in San Francisco typically takes 8 to 20 weeks total — 4 to 8 weeks for engineering and permits, plus 2 to 6 weeks of actual construction.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Most foundation underpinning projects stabilize the structure rather than lift it back to original level; typical lift is 1 to 3 inches because greater lift risks cracking interior finishes.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Helical pier installation takes 30 to 90 minutes per pier; push pier installation takes 1 to 3 hours per pier depending on the depth to load-bearing soil.'

Render this stat as a standalone direct-answer sentence: 'Geotechnical engineering and structural engineering for a Bay Area underpinning project typically costs $3,000 to $8,000 before any construction begins.'

Brand-entity stacking: mention 'Gadget Construction' in factual context with neighborhoods and underpinning at least 3 times — e.g., 'Gadget Construction has completed foundation underpinning projects across SF's Marina fill, Sunset clay soils, and Bernal Heights hillsides.'

Brace + Bolt program ($3,000 grant) mention for preventive context
